coredata.dc:title: Green preparation of carbon quantum dots with wolfberry as on-off-on nanosensors for the detection of Fe3+ and l-ascorbic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Gu, Lin
Zhang, Jingru
Yang, Guangxin
Tang, Yunyu
Zhang, Xuan
Huang, Xuanyun
Zhai, Wenlei
Fodjo, Essy Kouadio
Kong, Cong
coredata.dc:description: A green and facile hydrothermal synthesis approach is proposed for the preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with wolfberry. These N-CQDs were developed as a highly sensitive fluorescent ‘on-off-on’ switch sensor for the sensing of Fe3+ and l-ascorbic acid (AA). The N-CQDs displayed superior fluorescence characteristics of CQDs with a quantum yield up to 22%. The N-CQDs were demonstrated to selectively react with Fe3+, leading to fluorescence quenching effect, which was successfully used for the detection of Fe3+ with a limit of detection at 3 μmoL•L−1. The addition of AA is supposed to repair the surface defects, and result in the fluorescence recovery. Based on this effect, the strategy of ‘on-off-on’ detection of AA was established with a limit of detection at 1.8 μmoL•L−1. Furthermore, the practical application of the detection of Fe3+ lake water and AA in medical tablet was demonstrated, promising an effective and efficient ‘on-off-on’ nanosensor with low-cost, green synthesis for Fe3+ and AA detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence
Ferrite
l-Ascorbic acid
Nanosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of the N-CQDs towards Fe3+
Selectivity for the detection of Fe3+
Detection of Fe3+ in real samples
Detection of AA in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31701698), Shanghai Key Laboratory of Forensic Medicine (Academy of Forensic Science, KF1910), Central Public-interest Scientific Institution Basal Research Fund, CAFS (2020TD72), the special research fund for the national non-profit institutes (East China Sea Fisheries Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Fishery Sciences, China) (2016T09) and Basic Research Fund for Nonprofit Research Institutes in Fujian (Mindong Fisheries Research Institute of Fujian Province, China, 2021R1036-3).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of hydrothermal synthesis of N-CQDs from wolfberry.

(a) TEM micrograph of N-CQDs. (b) Particle size distribution of N-CQDs. (c) FTIR spectrum of the prepared N-CQDs. (d) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the N-CQDs. (e) Full scan XPS of N-CQDs. (f) High resolution and fitted spectrum of O1s. (g) High resolution and fitted spectrum of N1s. (h) High resolution and fitted spectrum of C1s.

(a) F/F0 under pH 6.0 in the presence of metal ions and amino acids (500 μmol•L−1) (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs (0.1 mg•mL−1) in different concentrations of Fe3+ ions ranging from 0 to 100 μmol•L−1. (c) The relationship between the concentration of Fe3+ and the fluorescence quench.
(a) Fluorescence ‘on-off-on’ after the addition of Fe3+ (129 μmol•L−1) and AA (100 μmol•L−1). (b) The relationship between the concentration of AA and the fluorescence recovering.

head.ce:title: Green preparation of carbon quantum dots with wolfberry as on-off-on nanosensors for the detection of Fe3+ and l-ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physical Chemistry Laboratory, UFR SSMT, Université Felix Houphouet Boigny, 22 BP 582 Abidjan 22, Cote d’Ivoire

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A green and facile hydrothermal synthesis approach is proposed for the preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with wolfberry. These N-CQDs were developed as a highly sensitive fluorescent ‘on-off-on’ switch sensor for the sensing of Fe3+ and l-ascorbic acid (AA). The N-CQDs displayed superior fluorescence characteristics of CQDs with a quantum yield up to 22%. The N-CQDs were demonstrated to selectively react with Fe3+, leading to fluorescence quenching effect, which was successfully used for the detection of Fe3+ with a limit of detection at 3 μmoL•L−1. The addition of AA is supposed to repair the surface defects, and result in the fluorescence recovery. Based on this effect, the strategy of ‘on-off-on’ detection of AA was established with a limit of detection at 1.8 μmoL•L−1. Furthermore, the practical application of the detection of Fe3+ lake water and AA in medical tablet was demonstrated, promising an effective and efficient ‘on-off-on’ nanosensor with low-cost, green synthesis for Fe3+ and AA detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are promising materials due to their simple preparation, low cost, good photoluminescence, low toxicity, and excitation wavelength dependence, which have attracted widespread attention in the past decade (Chen et al., 2017). CQDs are emerging nanomaterial with unique properties (Zhao & Chung, 2018), with diameters normally ranging from about 1–10 nm. Their surface generally contains abundant functional groups, such as carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups. These functional groups endow CQDs with good hydrophilicity (Hutton, Martindale, & Reisner, 2017). Because of these advantages, CQDs are widely used as fluorescent probes (Devi et al., 2017), photocatalysts (Wang, Lu, Tang, & Xu, 2017), adsorbents (Sabet & Mahdavi, 2019), nanocomposite membranes (Zeng et al., 2016), biomedicine (Wang et al., 2017) and wastewater detection agent (H.-Y. Li, Li, Guo, Yang, Wei, & Xie, 2018). Recently, CQDs have also been used as ‘on-off-on’ nanoprobes to detect harmful substances, Song et al. established an ‘on-off-on’ ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor to detect Zn2+ (Song et al., 2018).

The main synthesis methods of CQDs are divided into two categories. One is top-down, stripping carbon nanoparticles from large carbon structures, mainly including Arc-discharge method (Xu et al., 2004), laser ablation method (Sun et al., 2006) and electrochemical method (Sui, Wu, Zhong, & Liu, 2019), etc. The other one is a bottom-up strategy, which mainly includes thermal pyrolysis (Bourlinos, Stassinopoulos, Anglos, Zboril, Karakassides, & Giannelis, 2008), microwave (Che et al., 2019) and hydrothermal methods (Arumugam & Kim, 2018), etc al. The top-down method requires more expensive equipment and more tedious pretreatment. On the contrary, the bottom-up method involves simple pretreatment and lower cost, and gradually becoming a common strategy for preparing CQDs in recent years (Chen et al., 2017). The major advantages of the hydrothermal method include green, environmentally friendly, and low-cost for the preparation of CQDs. There is no need to add toxic and harmful chemicals during the preparation process (Li et al., 2014). Different precursors can be used to prepare CQDs by hydrothermal method. Even organic waste can be used to prepare CQDs by hydrothermal method, such as waste lemon peel (Tyagi, Tripathi, Singh, Choudhary, & Gupta, 2016) and grass (Sabet & Mahdavi, 2019), attaining the purpose of solid wastes utilization.

CQDs have been reported for its various prominent merits, but its low QY is frequently the shortcoming. Some toxic solvents like toluene are used to obtain high QY (Y. Liu, Zhang, Wang, Cui, & Song, 2017). Therefore, it is necessary to explore a green synthesis method to improve the QY of CQDs. Heteroatoms doping in CQDs can adjust the inherent properties of CQDs and result in high QY (Yuanyuan Liu, Jiang, Li, Fan, Wang, Liu, et al., 2019). N atoms have a similar atomic size to the carbon atoms in CQDs and can have 5 valence electrons to bond with carbon atoms. Consequently, N atoms are widely used in the doping of carbon nanomaterials (Du et al., 2015).

Wolfberry is a fruit of the perennial deciduous shrub as a common food-source materials, containing polysaccharides, sugars, betaine, various amino and carotenoids acids (Yajun et al., 2019). The planting area of wolfberry exceeds 1.33 × 105 ha in China. It is rich in N element naturally and very easy to obtain. That makes wolfberry an ideal carbon and nitrogen containing precursor. The N elements in wolfberry can be incorporated into CQDs through hydrothermal carbonization.

Due to their unique fluorescence characteristics, N-CQDs have potential applications in the detection of trace substances. Fe3+ is one of the necessary trace elements for humans, and it plays an important role in cell and biological metabolism (Hentze, Muckenthaler, Galy, & Camaschella, 2010). Its deficiency may cause anemia in the human body, and excessive Fe3+ can cause pressure on the liver, subsequently leading to a series of diseases such as liver cirrhosis (Brugnara, 2003). Moreover, the detection of Fe3+ in drinking water and water environment is of great significance. There are many detection methods for Fe3+ at this stage, in which electrochemistry, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry andatomic absorption spectrometer are the more common (Qi, Xu, Li, Chen, Xu, & Liu, 2021). However, these detection methods have higher requirements for instruments and processing. Thus, a simple and low-cost detection method for Fe3+ is of great importance.

AA, known as vitamin C, has a very important meaning and effect on the normal function of the human body. Lack of AA may cause anemia and excessive AA can cause gastritis. Therefore, the establishment of a simple and accurate method for AA detection is important to the early prevention of diseases caused by AA. CQDs have broad application prospects in the detection of AA. Gong et al. have successfully obtained CQDs from mixing glucose, 1,2-ethylenediamine and concentrated phosphoric. Due to the efficient chelation of the AA functional group and its reducibility, this CQDs have acted as a ‘turn-on’ fluorescence nanoprobe to detect AA after the addition of Cr6+ (Gong, Liu, Yang, Shuang, Zhang, & Dong, 2017).

In this study, a new method of preparing N-CQDs from wolfberry is explored and used for fluorescence detection of Fe3+ in drinking water. The limit of detection (LOD) is 3 μmol•L−1, lower than that obtained with the CQDs synthesized by Khan which LOD is 19 μmol•L−1 (Khan et al., 2017). And with upconversion characteristics, N-CQDs can detect Fe3+ under low-energy wavelength excitation. After reacting with Fe3+. This ion is transformated into Fe2+. In paralelle, surface defects formed on the N-CQDs. The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs decreases and AA is able to restore the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs + Fe3+ composite system by repairing the defects on N-CQDs. This process of reaction can be used as a novel stratedgy for ‘on-off-on’ detection of AA in tablets. Since the N-CQDs are prepared from wolfberry, it involves green synthesis, low cost and can be produced on a large scale. Overall this work demonstrates various applications of N-CQDs prepared from wolfberry.

Wolfberry was purchased from Ningxia (China). Metal salt: MnCl2·4H2O (99.0%), CdCl2·2.5H2O (99.0%), CoCl2·6H2O (99.0%), LiCl·H2O (97.0%), CaCl2 (96.0%), KCl (99.8%), HgCl2 (99.9%), AA and various amino acids were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Vitamin C tablets were produced by Shanghai Xinyi Tianping Pharmaceutical Co. Ltd. and Shanghai New Yellow River Pharmaceutical Co. Ltd. All chemicals are of analytical grade. Deionized water was used in the experiment.

N-CQDs were synthesized through a hydrothermal carbonization technique. Briefly, 2 g of wolfberry was added into 5 mL of deionized water in a steel reactor. This mixture was then transferred into an autoclave and carbonized for a certain time at a certain temperature. The autoclave was cooled down to room temperature and the synthesized material was washed with 5 mL of deionized water. And then, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min to remove large particles. The supernatant was filtered with 0.22 μm hydrophilic membrane, and further dialyzed in a dialysis bag for 24 h. Finally, N-CQDs were lyophilized and dry N-CQDs were obtained. The same batch of wolfberry was used in our different experiment, quality difference was not investigated in this work.
Quinine Sulfate (dispersed in 0.1 mol•L−1 H2SO4, QY = 54%) was used as Standard. The QY was calculated as follows:
Ψ = ΨR×(I/A)/(IR/AR)×(η/ηR)2

Among the formula, Ψ, I, and A are the QY, Sample integral intensity, and optical density, respectively. ΨR, IR, AR are quinine sulfate QY, integral intensity, and optical density, respectively. η is refractive index of quinine sulfate. ηR is the refractive index of the sample.

Characterizations of the N-CQDs include 200 KV FEI-Tecnai G2 20 S-TWIN High-resolution TEM for the detection of particle size of N-CQDs, fluorescence spectrometer (F97pro Fluorescence Spectroscopy, China) for the fluorescence test in which both excitation bandwidth and emission bandwidth are 10 nm, and UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV–Vis Hitachi u4100, Japan) for Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra acquisition. Besides, XPS (Kratos Axis Ultra DLD, Kratos, UK) was used to determine the elemental composition while FT-IR (Nicolet iS50 spectrometer via, China) was used to characterize the molecular structure of N-CQDs. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was obtained on a Bruker D8 Advance (Germany).

Fe3+ can quench the fluorescence of N-CQDs, the following step is the use of N-CQDs to detect Fe3+. 10 μL of Fe3+ solution with different concentrations (0.129 mmol•L−1–0.129 mol•L−1) was added into the N-CQDs solution (0.1 mg•mL−1). After mixing and leaving under reaction for one minute, its fluorescence was measured with the excitation wavelength of 350 nm and 673 nm, respectively. Several common interferences were used to explore the selective response of N-CQDs to Fe3+. The pH of all solution were adjusted to 6 by addition of phosphatebufferedsolution (pH = 6).

Two milliliters of the solution containing 65 μmol•L−1 of Fe3+ and N-CQDs (0.1 mg•mL−1) were injected into a cuvette. Different concentrations of AA were added into the N-CQDs + Fe3+ composite system. The pH of all solution were adjusted to 6 by addition of phosphatebufferedsolution (pH = 6). After six minutes of silence, the emission was measured at room temperature. All of the above tests were performed with excitation at 350 nm.

Water samples were obtained in Taihu Lake in Jiangsu, Qiannan Lake in Guizhou, from drinking water and tap water respectively. Firstly, the water samples was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min and filtered through the 0.22 μm hydrophilic membrane. Twenty microliters of N-CQDs (10 mg•mL−1) was injected into 1 mL of water samples and was further mixed with 1 mL of deionized water.

The feasibility of the method was evaluated through the test of vitamin C tablets. The tablet was dissolved in a 100 mL volumetric flask with deionized water before test. All of the above tests were performed using excitation wavelength at 350 nm. All samples were tested three times.

In order to optimize the synthesis process of N-CQDs, the temperature was kept constant at 180℃ for different reaction times. The fluorescence spectrum of N-CQDs (0.1 mg•mL−1) was measured under the excitation wavelength at 350 nm. As shown in Fig. S1 (a & b, supplementary information), the fluorescence intensity of the carbonized N-CQDs for 8 h is the highest, and the lowest fluorescence intensity was observed at 10 h. Therefore, the best carbonization time for N-CQDs preparation was set at 8 h.

Furthermore, the temperature for N-CQDs preparation was also examined from 140 to 220℃. As shown in Fig. S1 (c & d, supplementary information), the fluorescence intensity under carbonized temperature of 180℃ is the strongest with the gradually QY increasing below 180℃, followed by its decline after reaching 180℃. Therefore, 180 ℃ was chosen as the best carbonization temperature. The synthesized N-CQDs from wolfberry under carbonization temperature was set at 180℃ for 8 h produces QY of 0.22.

N-CQDs were prepared through hydrothermal treatment of wolfberry as schemed in Fig. 1 . The morphology and size distribution acquired through TEM micrograph of N-CQDs are shown in Fig. 2 (a & b) The formation of small spherical particles with an average diameter in the range of 1.55 ± 1 nm was observed, indicating the formation of N-CQDs. XRD was carried out for structural analysis. Fig. S2 (supplementary information) shows that the broad peak at 2θ = 20.9。 corresponds to the (002) plane of graphite (Latief, Ul Islam, Khan, & Khan, 2021).

FTIR spectrum in Fig. 2 (c) shows the absorption peaks at around 1600, 1350, 1080 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of CC/CO, CO, CN, respectively. The broad peak at 3450 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH/NH, demonstrating the doping of nitrogen on the N-CQDs. The stretching vibration of CH is at 2810 cm−1. The existence of CO in N-CQDs improves the hydrophilicity of N-CQDs, promising great potential in water sample testing.

Due to n-π* transition of CO and π-π* transition of CC (Yang et al., 2014), the N-CQDs shows a strong absorption at 270 nm as displayed in Fig. 2 (d). The highest excitation and emission are found at the wavelength of 350 nm and 425 nm, respectively. It should be noted that these CQDs emit blue light under a 360 nm ultraviolet lamp, indicating its potential biomedical applications (Rong, Feng, Wang, & Chen, 2017).

In order to confirm the various groups present on the N-CQDs, the XPS measurement is applied. Fig. 2 (e) shows the full scan XPS spectra with three distinct strong peaks at 285 eV, 400 eV, 531 eV, corresponding to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively (Yang, Wang, Luo, Feng, Peng, & Liao, 2020). Fig. 2 (f) exhibits three peaks at 530.9 eV, 531.6 eV and 532.6 eV, corresponding to CO, COH and COC bonds, respectively. Fig. 2 (g) shows three peaks present at 399.7 eV, 400.5 eV, 399.2 eV, corresponding to CNC, N-C3, CN-C. While the C 1 s spectra in Fig. 2 (h) at 284.6 eV, 285.7 eV, 285.9 eV corresponds to CC, CC, CN bond. In addition, the N-CQDs have abundant hydroxyl groups and other hydrophilic groups on their surface, leading to a good hydrophilicity, which is consistent with the measurement results of the infrared spectrum.

Fig. S3 (a, supplementary information) shows the fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs (0.1 mg•mL−1) under different excitation wavelengths from 310 to 390 nm. The fluorescence intensity reaches maximum under the excitation of 350 nm with an emission peak at 425 nm. With the increase of the excitation wavelength, the emission peaks shift towards longer wavelength, and the fluorescence intensity increases, followed by a decrease. This result shows that the N-CQDs have excitation-dependent emission. This phenomenon can be attributed to the different surface organic groups on the N-CQDs (Khan et al., 2019). Thus, the subsequent fluorescence intensity was measured under the excitation wavelength at 350 nm. Fig. S3 (b, supplementary information) shows the fluorescence through upconversion excitation wavelength at 673 nm, promising its usability in subsequent tests.

A high-concentration of N-CQDs (0.4 mg•mL−1) was prepared to confirm its luminescence range under an excitation wavelength ranging from 300 to 530 nm. The result in Fig. S4 (supplementary information) shows that the N-CQDs have fluorescence intensity in the excitation wavelength range, and the fluorescence intensity increases for wavelength less than 380 nm, followed by a decrease, indicating that the N-CQDs has a wide luminous range, from purple to yellow.

The N-CQDs of the same concentration (0.1 mg•mL−1) were adjusted into different pH values, to assess their effect on the fluorescence. Fig. S5 (a, supplementary information) shows that the fluorescence intensity reaches the maximum at pH = 6. The intensity decreases when the pH changed to both acidic or alkaline environment. At pH = 13, the degree of fluorescence quenching is the strongest, indicating that the protonation, and non-protonation under over-acidic or over-alkaline environment leads to fluorescence quenching effect (Wu et al., 2013). Therefore, in the subsequent measurement, the pH of the N-CQDs solution was adjusted to 6.

Different concentrations of NaCl were added into the N-CQDs (0.1 mg•mL−1) solution to further evaluate the effect of the ionic strength on the N-CQDs fluorescence. The result is shown in Fig. S5 (b, supplementary information). The increase of the ionic strength does not affect the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs, which means that the N-CQDs surfaces have almost no ionization of the groups located on the surface (Zhang et al., 2014).

In order to investigate the influence of external factors on the stability of N-CQDs, N-CQDs solution (0.1 mg•mL−1) was placed in a xenon lamp for different times to measure their fluorescence intensity. Fig. S5 (c, supplementary information) shows that fluorescence intensity can be kept at a constant level, indicating its good light stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of the N-CQDs towards Fe3+
Selectivity for the detection of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: N-CQDs are often used as fluorescent probes for heavy metal ions due to their excellent optical properties and luminescence properties (Rani, Ng, Ng, & Mahmoudi, 2020). Good selectivity is an important indicator of sensors. A series of heavy metal ions, such as Mn2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Li+, Ca2+, K+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+and Cu2+ were used to evaluate the selectivity of N-CQDs. Different metal salt ions were dissolved in deionized water, and all the prepared ions concentration was 0.1 mol•L−1. Buffer solution was added to make the pH of the N-CQDs solution (0.1 mg•mL−1) at 6. After mixing, its fluorescence was measured with excitation at 350 nm. Some typical amino acids, such as Lys, Arg, Glu, Phe, Pro, Cys, Val, Ile were also examined. The fluorescence intensity before and after the addition of 10 μL metal ions and amino acids is recorded as F0 and F, respectively. The results of F/F0 are shown in Fig. 3 (a) and Table S1 (supplementary information).

Apart from Fe3+, the F/F0 had no significant change after the addition of metal ions and amino acids. The result indicates that N-CQDs have good chemical stability and selectivity. After adding 10 μL of Fe3+ (0.1 mol•L−1), the fluorescence intensity drops to 30% of the initial value, indicating potential capability of N-CQDs in the detection of Fe3+.

In order to explore the sensitivity of this method, different concentrations of Fe3+ ranging from 0 to 100 μmol•L−1 were added to N-CQDs solution with pH at 6. The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs decreased, and reached a stable state after 1 min (Fig. S6, supplementary information) under the excitation at 350 nm. From Fig. 3 (b), it is clearly that with the increase in the concentration of Fe3+ ions, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases. In the range of 6–100 μmol•L−1, the degree of quenching has a good linear relationship with the concentration of Fe3+ (Fig. 3 (c) inset). The LOD is calculated based on LOD = 3δ/K (Qiao, Lu, Tang, Gao, & Wang, 2019), where δ represents the standard deviation, K is the slope of the standard curve. The calculated detection limit is 3 μmol•L−1. Recovery by spiking experiment ranges from 93% to 108%, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 5.2% (n = 6), demonstrating the reliability of N-CQDs for the detection of Fe3+. Furthermore, Table S2 (supplementary information) listed a comparison of the LOD for the detection of Fe3+ with the earlier reported literature. The LOD of this method was found to be relatively lower.

N-CQDs have been reported with good upconversion (Duo, Wang, Fan, Zhang, & Wang, 2016). In our experiment, the emission of N-CQDs at 410 nm under excitation at 673 nm was observed. After adding Fe3+ into N-CQDs, the fluorescence intensity decreases and reaches a stable state within one minute. A higher fluorescence quenching degree is observed with the increase of Fe3+ ion concentration, as shown in Fig. S7 (supplementary information). The degree of fluorescence quenching has a good linear relationship with Fe3+ concentration between 3 and 100 μmol•L−1 (Fig. S7 inset, supplementary information) with a calculated detection limit of 3 μmol•L−1 which is similar to the limit of detection obtained using shorter excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Therefore, the use of low-energy light to excite N-CQDs in the Fe3+ detection can be achieved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the practicability of the fluorescent probe, four water samples from Taihu Lake in Jiangsu, Qiannan Guizhou, drinking water, and tap water were collected for testing. The experiment was performed in triplicates under the same conditions to obtain the RSD. It can be seen from Table S3 (supplementary information) that the Fe3+ content in drinking water sample is the lowest. However, Fe3+ content of Taihu Lake is higher than that of Qiannan, Guizhou. The RSDs are lower than 15%. The above results demonstrated the feasibility of N-CQDs in the detection of Fe3+ in real samples.
After the addition of AA into N-CQDs + Fe3+ system, the fluorescence intensity is recovered (Fig. 4 (a)). Based on this process, the N-CQDs-Fe3+ system can be applied to detect AA in tablets.

With the addition of AA, the fluorescence of N-CQDs/Fe3+/AA mixture is gradually strengthened (Fig. S8, supplementary information). The fluorescence intensity reaches stability after 6 min (Fig. S9, supplementary information). From 10 to 100 μmol•L−1, there is an excellent linear relationship between F/F0 and the AA concentration (Fig. 4(b)). The LOD is calculated to be 1.8 μmol•L−1. This ‘off-on’ fluorescence nanoprobe based on N-CQDs/Fe3+ has the advantages of simplicity and sensitivity. Recovery by spiking experiment ranges from 86% to 112%, with a relative standard deviation of less than 9.6% (n = 6). These results show that the N-CQDs are reliable when applying on real samples. In effort to show the sensibility of AA detection, Table S4 (supplementary information) lists comparison of the LOD for the detection of AA with the earlier reported literature. The LOD of this method was relatively lower.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application of the mixed system for the determination of AA was verified by its application in vitamin tablets. The experiment was performed triplicate under the same conditions to get the relative errors (REs) and the RSD. Table S5 (supplementary information) compares known amounts against the experimental results. The REs ranged from 11.40% to 15.30%, while RSDs were lower than 7%. This result demonstrated that the N-CQDs can be used in the detection of AA in real samples.

In order to explore the mechanism of observed fluorescebce ‘off-on’ effect during the tests, UV–vis absorption of Fe3+ (64.5 μmol•L−1) added in 2 mL N-CQDs (0.1 mg•mL−1) were measured (Fig. S10, supplementary information). As shown in this figure, the absorbance of Fe3+ is reduced after the addition of N-CQDs. It can be inferred that all or part of Fe3+ is transformed into other substances after the reaction, resulting in a decrease in the UV absorption peak.

To verify the products from the reaction of Fe3+ and N-CQDs, o-phenanthroline (Fe2+ specific indicator) was introduced, and the reacted solution turned red, indicating that Fe2+ was formed during the reaction. Different dilutions of the Fe3+ solution were added to 5 mL N-CQDs (0.1 mg•mL−1), and subsequently, 1 mL of 1% o-phenanthroline aqueous solution were added to the 12 test tubes, respectively. As shown in Fig. S11 (supplementary information), the color change in the diluted solution of N-CQDs and Fe3+ iron ions did not occur. However, the mixed solution of N-CQDs and Fe3+ turned red after the addition of the o-phenanthroline aqueous solution. With the increase of Fe3+ ion concentration, the color changes from light red to deep red, indicating that Fe2+ is formed after Fe3+ was added into N-CQDs. When the concentration of N-CQDs is sufficient, the concentration of Fe2+ ions produced by the reaction increases upon the addition of Fe3+.

In order to figure out the principle of fluorescence recovery after the addition of AA, the fluorescence of N-CQDs after the addition of Fe3+ (645 μmol•L−1), Fe2+ (645 μmol•L−1), AA (100 μmol•L−1) in different order was measured (Fig. S12, supplementary information). The fluorescence of N-CQDs did not change when AA was first added, no matter Fe3+ (Fig. S12 (b), supplementary information) or Fe2+ (Fig. S12 (d), supplementary information) was added subsequently. This phenomenon indicated that AA provides a reductive environment, which leads to the failure of Fe3+ to form surface defects on N-CQDs.

The reaction of N-CQDs with Fe3+ may cause the fluorescence quenching. The electrons on the N-CQDs under light excitation, undergo transitions. The excited state electrons are unstable and tend to return to the ground state (Lim, Shen, & Gao, 2015). After adding Fe3+, Fe2+ was produced, and the surface defects on N-CQDs formed in paralelle, resulting in the failure of transistions of exited electron to the ground state. Finally, the fluorescence quenching occurs on the surface of N-CQDs. Furthermore, the addition of AA repaired these surface defects and recovered the transitions of excited electrons.

According to our experiment, the fluorescence ‘on-off-on’ principle of the N-CQDs is shown in Fig. 5 . The fluorescence of N-CQDs is turned off due to the failure of electron transfer on N-CQDs after addition of Fe3+. After the addition of AA, the defects state on N-CQDs is repaired. As a result, the fluorescence is turn ‘on’.

In summary, wolfberry was used as a green carbon and nitrogen source to prepare N-doped carbon quantum dots with excellent fluorescence through a hydrothermal method. Based on the fluorescent ‘on-off’ mechanism, the N-CQDs were successfully used as a selective probe for Fe3+ detection. Besides, the fluorescence ‘off-on’ system has been successfully applied to detect AA. The principle of the N-CQDs in the detection of Fe3+ and AA is due to the selective reaction of Fe3+ with N-CQDs leading to surface defects, which can be repared by the addition of AA. Compared with classic nanosensors, the N-CQDs as ‘on-off-on’ probe is easy to prepare, harmless, and has good selectivity and high sensitivity. It has been successfully demonstrated in the detection of Fe3+ in lake water and the detection of AA in medicines, promising good feasibility in water quality monitoring of Fe3+ and AA involved testing.

Lin Gu: Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation. Jingru Zhang: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Guangxin Yang: Conceptualization, Resources, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition, Methodology. Yunyu Tang: Validation, Data curation. Xuan Zhang: Validation. Xuanyun Huang: Visualization, Resources. Wenlei Zhai: Writing – review & editing. Essy Kouadio Fodjo: Writing – review & editing. Cong Kong: Conceptualization, Resources, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31701698), Shanghai Key Laboratory of Forensic Medicine (Academy of Forensic Science, KF1910), Central Public-interest Scientific Institution Basal Research Fund, CAFS (2020TD72), the special research fund for the national non-profit institutes (East China Sea Fisheries Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Fishery Sciences, China) (2016T09) and Basic Research Fund for Nonprofit Research Institutes in Fujian (Mindong Fisheries Research Institute of Fujian Province, China, 2021R1036-3).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.131898.
